14: The regnal evaluation of Asa — wᵉlēḇ ʾāsāʾ hāyāh šālēm ʿim YHWH kol yāmāyw, ‘Asa’s heart was wholly true (šālēm) to YHWH all his days’ — is the Deuteronomistic historian’s highest royal verdict. The adjective šālēm (whole, complete, undivided, at peace) is the root of šālôm (peace, well-being) and of Solomon’s name (šᵉlōmōh): the lēḇ šālēm is the heart that has found its integration and rest in YHWH alone, undivided by rival loyalties. 2 Chronicles 16:9 extends the principle as a divine promise: ‘the eyes of YHWH run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to give strong support to those whose heart is šālēm toward him.’ The regnal evaluations of Kings and Chronicles use the šālēm standard as their binary — each king either maintains wholeness or fragments it. Asa himself fails this standard in 2 Chronicles 16:7-12, where he trusts in the king of Aram rather than YHWH in his final years, and his heart ceases to be šālēm. The NT appropriation of the whole-heart standard: Deuteronomy 6:5 (‘you shall love YHWH your God with all your heart, bᵉkol lᵉḇāḇᵉḵā’) is what Jesus identifies in Matthew 22:37 as the first and greatest commandment — the lēḇ šālēm as the definition of the covenant’s core demand. The fact that no king fully sustained the lēḇ šālēm across an entire reign is the OT’s implied argument for why the Davidic promise requires a greater son.
1: Aḇîyām (H38) vs. the parallel form Aḇîyāh (Chronicles) — both names mean 'my father is YHWH,' but 1 Kings consistently uses the -yām ending, possibly a textual variant or scribal preference. The regnal formula situates him in the synchronistic chronology: eighteenth year of Jeroboam son of Nebat, maintaining the dual-kingdom reckoning throughout the divided-monarchy narrative.
2: šālōš šānîm mālaḵ bîrûšālāim — three years, the shortest reign listed for any Davidic king until Ahaziah. His mother (ʾimmô) is Maacah daughter of Abishalom — the same woman who later appears as the gᵉḇîrāh (queen mother) in 1 Kings 15:13. The Abishalom/Absalom identification is debated; she may be a granddaughter of David's son Absalom.
3: wayyēleḵ bᵉḵol-ḥaṭṭōʾt ʾāḇîw — he walked in all the sins of his father. The verb hālaḵ bᵉ- (to walk in/after) is the standard Deuteronomistic evaluative formula for apostasy; the preposition bᵉ signals immersion in a pattern of behavior, not single acts. Lᵉḇāḇô (his heart) qualified by šālēm (whole, complete) appears in 1 Kings 15:14 as the contrast: Abijam's heart was not šālēm ʿim YHWH as his father David's was.
4: nîr (H5216) — often rendered 'lamp,' but the context here is dynastic: YHWH gives David a nîr in Jerusalem, meaning an heir-maintained flame of royal continuity. The term recurs in 11:36 and 15:4 as a covenant pledge. Hāqîm ʾeṯ-bᵉnô ʾaḥărāyw — raising up his son after him: the language of 2 Samuel 7:12 (the Davidic covenant), explicitly invoked here to explain why an unworthy king still holds the throne.
5: The parenthetical exception — raq bᵉḏaḇar ʾûrîyāh haḥittî, 'except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite' — is the Deuteronomistic historian's only qualifying note on David's otherwise perfect record. The phrasing 'the Hittite' distinguishes Uriah by ethnicity and marks the crime as involving a non-Israelite loyal warrior, compounding the moral weight. This exception clause protects the absolute Davidic standard while acknowledging historical reality.
6: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-rᵉḥaḇʿām ûḇên-yārāḇʿām — war existed between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout Abijam's entire life (kol-yᵉmê ḥayyāyw). This is a redactional resumption of the conflict note already made in 1 Kings 14:30. The northern–southern border conflict defines the background of the Abijam and early Asa periods.
7: The regnal closing formula: wᵉyeter diḇrê ʾaḇîyām wᵉḵol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh — the rest of Abijam's acts and all he did are in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. This archival reference appears stereotypically throughout the divided monarchy; it implies a source document beyond the biblical text, now lost. The formula signals narrative closure before the accession notice.
8: wayyiškaḇ ʾaḇîyām ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw — the sleep-with-ancestors idiom for natural death, as opposed to 'he died' (wayyāmot), which may imply violent death. Burial in the city of David (ʿîr dāwîd) marks him as within the legitimate Davidic line despite his failure; David's tomb serves as the royal burial standard for Judah.
9: bišnat ʿeśrîm lᵉyārāḇʿām — synchronistic dating: Asa begins in Jeroboam's twentieth year. The dual-kingdom dating system requires the historian to maintain two parallel chronologies, cross-referenced at each accession. Asa's reign will span 41 years and overlap the reigns of six northern kings.
10: ʾaḇ-ʾimmô māʿăḵāh bat-ʾăḇîšālôm — his 'father's mother' (grandmother) was Maacah daughter of Abishalom, the same woman named as Abijam's mother in 1 Kings 15:2. This likely reflects the same person serving as gᵉḇîrāh through two reigns, a well-attested ANE practice where the queen mother position was inherited. The Hebrew ʾēm can mean biological mother or functionary in the queen-mother role.
11: wayyaʿaś ʾāsāʾ hayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉdāwîd ʾāḇîw — the positive Davidic standard: Asa did what was right in YHWH's eyes, like David his father. This is the highest evaluative grade in the Deuteronomistic system. Only Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah receive unqualified positive evaluations; Asa's is qualified in 1 Kings 15:14 by the high-places caveat.
12: wayyaʿaḇer haqqᵉdēšîm min-hāʾāreṣ — he expelled the qᵉdēšîm (H6945, masculine plural from qādôš) from the land. The term denotes male devotees associated with illicit cultic practice; the exact nature of their service remains debated (sacred prostitution vs. other cultic roles), but their association with non-YHWH worship is clear from the surrounding context. Wayyāsar ʾeṯ-kol-haggillulîm — and he removed all the gillulîm (H1544, 'dung-idols,' a pejorative for divine images). The root may relate to gālal (dung/rolling) — a deliberate slur against idol cults.
13: Already covered (v14 entry exists); this note is for v13: gᵉḇîrāh (H1377) — queen mother. The term is a semi-official title: the highest-ranking woman in the royal court, not merely a biological relation. Maacah's removal (wayyᵉsirēhā min-gᵉḇîrāh) for making a mipleset (H4656, a repulsive/abominable image) for Asherah is significant: this is the only biblical instance of a queen mother being deposed. Wayyiḵrōṯ ʾeṯ-mipleset and burning it at the Kidron valley — Naḥal qiḏrôn was the ritual dump for Jerusalem's cultic refuse.
15: wayyāḇēʾ ʾeṯ-qāḏᵉšê ʾāḇîw wᵉʾeṯ-qāḏᵉšāyw — he brought (bôʾ in hiphil) the things his father had dedicated (qāḏᵉšê ʾāḇîw) and the things he himself dedicated (qāḏᵉšāyw) into the house of YHWH. Qāḏᵉšîm here (different from 1 Kings 15:12) means 'dedicated/sacred things' — gold, silver, vessels. This marks Asa as a temple-restorationist, consistent with his overall positive evaluation.
16: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-ʾāsāʾ ûḇên-baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl kol-yᵉmêhem — war existed between Asa and Baasha king of Israel throughout their days. The resumptive formula echoes 1 Kings 15:6 and 1 Kings 14:30. This border warfare defines the political backdrop for Asa's appeal to Ben-hadad in 1 Kings 15:18–20.
17: wayyaʿal baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl ʿal-yᵉhûḏāh wayyiḇen ʾeṯ-hārāmāh — Baasha marched up against Judah and built (bānāh) Ramah. Ramah (5 miles north of Jerusalem) sat astride the main north–south route; fortifying it would have strangled Jerusalem's trade and communication. Lᵉḇiltî tēt lāṣēt wālāḇôʾ lᵉʾāsāʾ — so that no one could leave or enter for Asa — describes a blockade function, not just a military fortification.
18: wayyiqqaḥ ʾāsāʾ ʾeṯ-kol-haḵkesef wᵉhazzāhāḇ — Asa took all the silver and gold from the treasuries of the house of YHWH and the house of the king and sent them to Ben-hadad. The emptying of the temple treasury for a military alliance is a recurring pattern in the Judean monarchy (cf. 1 Kings 14:26); here it is strategic desperation. Ḇên-hăḏaḏ ben-ṭaḇrimmôn — Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon, the Aramean king of Damascus. This is the first appearance of Ben-hadad I in the narrative.
19: bᵉrît bênî ûḇêneḵā — 'a covenant between me and you,' invoking a prior treaty between fathers. The bᵉrît language is politically freighted: it implies loyalty obligations. Asa sends the silver as a gift (šōḥad, bribe/tribute) to break Ben-hadad's existing treaty with Baasha — he is buying a realignment of Aramean foreign policy.
20: wayyišmaʿ ben-hăḏaḏ ʾel-hammelek ʾāsāʾ — Ben-hadad complied. He sent his army commanders against the cities of Israel, striking ʿîyyôn, dān, ʾāḇēl bêṯ-maʿăḵāh, and all Kinnereth — the northern Galilee region. This Aramean intervention into northern Israel's border region forced Baasha to abandon the Ramah project.
21: wayyihy kišmōaʿ baʿšāʾ wayyeḥdal miḇnôṯ ʾeṯ-hārāmāh — when Baasha heard, he stopped building Ramah and returned to Tirzah. The verb ḥādal (H2308, to cease/stop) marks the military-political success of Asa's diplomatic strategy. Tirzah was the northern kingdom's capital from Jeroboam to Omri.
22: wayyašmiʿ hammelek ʾāsāʾ kol-yᵉhûḏāh ʾên nāqî — Asa summoned all Judah, no one exempt (ʾên nāqî, no one released/innocent of the levy). This is a labor conscription (mas ʿōḇēḏ) applied to the entire male population. They removed the stones and timber Baasha had used at Ramah and built with them Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah — recycling enemy fortification material for Judean frontier defense.
23: The closing formula for Asa: wᵉḵol-gᵉḇûrāṯô, all his might, plus the note that in his old age (bᵉʿēṯ ziḵnāṯô) he was diseased in his feet (ḥālāh ʾeṯ-raglāyw). The foot disease may be gout or arteriosclerosis; 2 Chronicles 16:12 adds that he sought physicians rather than YHWH, a theological note absent here in Kings.
24: wayyiškaḇ ʾāsāʾ ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqqāḇēr ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw bᵉʿîr dāwîd ʾāḇîw — Asa rested with his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David his father. The burial formula confirms dynastic continuity: even qualified kings receive the Davidic burial honor. His son Jehoshaphat reigned in his place — the succession formula (wayyimloḵ yᵉhôšāpāṭ bᵉnô taḥtāyw).
25: wᵉnāḏāḇ ben-yārāḇʿām mālaḵ ʿal-yiśrāʾēl bišnat šᵉtayim lᵉʾāsāʾ — Nadab son of Jeroboam became king of Israel in Asa's second year, reigning two years. The synchronistic dating resumes for the northern kingdom. Nadab's brief reign anticipates the pattern of short, violent tenures that characterize the northern monarchy after Jeroboam.
26: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyēleḵ bᵉḏereḵ ʾāḇîw ûḇᵉḥaṭṭāʾṯô — the negative Jeroboamic evaluation formula, applied here to Nadab. Walking in the way (dereḵ) of his father and in the sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt) by which Jeroboam caused Israel to sin — this phrase recurs verbatim for every unqualified northern king, a Deuteronomistic refrain anchoring the entire narrative of northern failure to the founding apostasy.
27: wayyiqšor ʿālāyw baʿšāʾ ben-ʾăḥîyāh lᵉḇêṯ-yiśāśḵār — Baasha son of Ahijah from the tribe of Issachar conspired (qāšar, H7194, to bind/conspire) against Nadab. The verb qāšar is the standard term for palace coups in Kings; its occurrence marks the fragile politics of the northern monarchy. Gibbethon was a Philistine-held town on the Dan–Ephraim border, where Israel was engaged in siege when the coup occurred — the army was away from the capital.
28: wayyamîṯēhû baʿšāʾ bišnat šālōš lᵉʾāsāʾ — Baasha killed him in Asa's third year and reigned in his place. The dynastic shift from Jeroboam's house to Baasha's is complete in a single verse. The brutal efficiency of the coup — striking during an active siege — is a realistic political-military detail.
29: wayyᵉhî kᵉmalḵô hikkāh ʾeṯ-kol-bêṯ yārāḇʿām lōʾ hišʾîr kol-nᵉšāmāh lᵉyārāḇʿām — as soon as he became king, he struck down the entire house of Jeroboam, leaving not one soul (nᵉšāmāh, H5397). The term nᵉšāmāh (breath/soul/living being) here means 'a single living person' — total dynastic elimination. The hiphil of šāʾar (leave behind) with lōʾ is the standard formula for total annihilation of a royal house (cf. 1 Kings 16:11).
30: ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾṯ yārāḇʿām ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ waʾăšer heḥᵉṭîʾ ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl — the theological rationale: because of the sins Jeroboam sinned and caused Israel to sin — the double accusation (ḥāṭāʾ and heḥᵉṭîʾ hiphil) marks both personal guilt and corporate causation. The phrase bᵉkaʿᵃsô ʾăšer hiḵʿîs ʾeṯ-YHWH — by the provocation with which he provoked YHWH — introduces kaʿas (H3708, vexation/anger-provocation), a term for idolatry-induced divine anger.
31: The archival closing formula for Nadab: wᵉyeter diḇrê nāḏāḇ wᵉḵol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh — the rest of Nadab's acts and all he did are written in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The reference to a northern chronicles document parallels the Judean source in 1 Kings 15:7; both are now lost but imply court records underlying the Deuteronomistic account.
32: Resumptive war notice: mîlḥāmāh hāyᵉṯāh bên-ʾāsāʾ ûḇên-baʿšāʾ melek-yiśrāʾēl kol-yᵉmêhem — war between Asa and Baasha all their days. This repetition of 1 Kings 15:16 brackets the Nadab episode and resumes the Asa–Baasha narrative thread. Repetitive resumption is a characteristic feature of the Deuteronomistic compositional technique.
33: bišnat šālōš lᵉʾāsāʾ melek yᵉhûḏāh mālaḵ baʿšāʾ ben-ʾăḥîyāh ʿal-kol-yiśrāʾēl — Baasha's accession in Asa's third year at Tirzah (ṯirṣāh), ruling the full (kol) Israel. The 24-year reign is the longest of any non-Omride northern king. Tirzah (modern Tell el-Farah North) served as the northern capital until Omri founded Samaria in 1 Kings 16:24.
34: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyēleḵ bᵉḏereḵ yārāḇʿām — the same Jeroboamic evaluation formula applied to Baasha. The irony is sharp: Baasha was raised up to judge Jeroboam's house (1 Kings 16:2–3) but walks in the same sin he was sent to punish. This pattern — instrument of judgment becoming the next subject of judgment — is a Deuteronomistic theological motif.
